It is not new for those whom Christ loves to be sick; bodily illnesses correct corruption and test the qualities of God’s people. He did not come to protect his people from these afflictions, but to save them from their sins and from the wrath to come. However, it is important that we pray to Him on behalf of our friends and relatives when they are sick and suffering. Let this reconcile us to the most difficult events in God’s plan, because they all serve to glorify God: sickness, loss, disappointment—all of these do. And if God is glorified, we should be content. Jesus loved Martha, her sister, and Lazarus. Families are greatly blessed when love and peace abound; but those are happiest whom Jesus loves and who love him in return. It is sad that this is not often the case with everyone, even in small families. God has good intentions, even when he seems to delay. When a work of deliverance—whether temporal or spiritual, public or personal—is delayed, it simply awaits the right time.

Christ never brings his people into danger without going with them through it. We are quick to think ourselves zealous for the Lord, but often we are only zealous for our wealth, reputation, comfort, and safety; therefore, we need to examine our beliefs. But your day will be lengthened out until your work is done and your testimony finished. A person has comfort and satisfaction while following the path of duty as set forth in God’s word and determined by God’s providence. Christ, wherever he went, walked in the daylight; and so shall we if we follow his steps. If a person walks according to their heart and the ways of this world, consulting their own desires more than the will and glory of God, they fall into temptations and snares. They stumble because there is no light within them; for light within us is to our moral actions what light around us is to our physical actions.

Since we are certain to rise again at the last, why should not the believing hope of that resurrection to eternal life make it as easy for us to put off the body and die as it is to take off our clothes and go to sleep? A true Christian, when they die, simply sleeps; they rest from the labors of the past day. Indeed, death is even better than sleep, because sleep is only a short rest, but death ends all earthly cares and struggles. The disciples thought that it was unnecessary for Christ to go to Lazarus and expose himself and them. We often hope that the good work we are called to do will be done by someone else if there is danger in doing it. But when Christ raised Lazarus from the dead, many came to believe in him; and much was accomplished to strengthen the faith of those who believed. Let us go to him; death cannot separate from the love of Christ, nor put us out of his reach. Like Thomas, Christians should encourage one another in difficult times. The Lord Jesus’s willingness to die should make us willing to die whenever God calls us.

This was a house where the fear of God was present and on which his blessing rested; yet it became a house of mourning. Grace will keep sorrow from the heart, but not from the house. When God, by his grace and providence, is coming towards us in ways of mercy and comfort, we should, like Martha, go forth with faith, hope, and prayer to meet him. When Martha went to meet Jesus, Mary remained at home; this temperament had previously been an advantage for her, as it led her to sit at Christ’s feet to hear his word. But in a time of affliction, the same temperament caused her to be melancholy. It is wise for us to watch against temptations and to use our natural tendencies for good. When we do not know what specifically to ask or expect, we should commit ourselves to God; let him do as he sees fit. To expand Martha’s expectations, our Lord declared himself to be the Resurrection and the Life. In every sense, he is the Resurrection: the source, the substance, the first fruits, the cause of it. The redeemed soul lives after death in happiness; and after the resurrection, both body and soul are kept from all evil forever. When we have read or heard the word of Christ about the great things of the other world, we should ask ourselves, Do we believe this truth? The crosses and comforts of this present time would not affect us so deeply if we believed the truths of eternity as we ought to. When Christ our Master comes, he calls for us. He comes in his word and ordinances, and calls us to them; he calls us by them; he calls us to himself. Those who, in a time of peace, sit at Christ’s feet to be taught by him may, with comfort, in a time of trouble, cast themselves at his feet to find favor with him.

Christ’s tender sympathy with these afflicted friends was shown by the distress of his spirit. In all the afflictions of believers he is afflicted. His concern for them was demonstrated by his kind inquiry about the remains of his deceased friend. Being found in human form, he acts as people do. It was shown by his tears. He was a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief. Tears of compassion resemble those of Christ. But Christ never approved of that sensitivity which many are proud of, while they weep at mere stories of distress but are hardened to real suffering. He sets us an example to withdraw from scenes of frivolous joy so that we may comfort the afflicted. And we do not have a High Priest who cannot be touched by our weaknesses. It is a good step toward raising a soul to spiritual life when the stone is removed, when prejudices are overcome, and the way is made for the word to enter the heart. If we take Christ’s word and rely on his power and faithfulness, we will see the glory of God and be happy in his presence. Our Lord Jesus has taught us by his own example to call God Father in prayer and to draw near to him as children to a father, with humble reverence yet with bold confidence. He openly addressed God in this way, with uplifted eyes and a loud voice, so that they might be convinced that the Father had sent him as his beloved Son into the world. He could have raised Lazarus by the silent exertion of his power and will, and the unseen working of the Spirit of life; but he did it by a loud call. This was a symbol of the gospel call, by which dead souls are brought out of the grave of sin, and of the sound of the archangel’s trumpet at the last day, with which all who sleep in the dust will be awakened and summoned before the great tribunal. The grave of sin and this world is no place for those whom Christ has quickened; they must come forth. Lazarus was thoroughly revived and returned not only to life but also to health. A sinner cannot quicken his own soul, but he should use the means of grace; a believer cannot sanctify himself, but he should lay aside every weight and hindrance. We cannot convert our relatives and friends, but we should instruct, warn, and invite them.

There can hardly be a more clear indication of the madness that is in people’s hearts and their desperate opposition to God than what is recorded here. Words of prophecy spoken are not clear evidence of a principle of grace in the heart. The calamity we seek to escape by sin, we bring upon ourselves; as those do who think they will advance their own worldly interests by opposing Christ's kingdom. The fear of the wicked will come upon them. The conversion of souls is the gathering of them to Christ as their ruler and refuge; and he died to accomplish this. By dying, he purchased them to himself, and the gift of the Holy Spirit for them: his love in dying for believers should unite them closely together.

Before our Passover celebration we must renew our repentance. Thus, by voluntary purification and religious exercises, many, more devout than their neighbors, spent some time before the Passover at Jerusalem. When we expect to meet God, we must solemnly prepare. No human schemes can alter God’s purposes; and while hypocrites amuse themselves with forms and arguments, and worldly people pursue their own plans, Jesus still orders all things for his own glory and the salvation of his people.
